explanation: When you talk about something you do every morning, you use the present indicative for the habit. Regular -are verbs build the io form from the stem plus -o: compr- + -o gives compro.

translation.reveal: guardare - to watch / to look at

explanation: The tu form of a regular -are verb is the stem plus -i. From guardare, drop -are and add -i: guardi. The ending -i marks the second person singular.
For he or she, a regular -are verb takes the stem plus -a in the present indicative: abit- + -a gives abita. Note the -a ending belongs to the first conjugation; the other classes use -e here.
The noi form across every regular class ends in -iamo. From parlare you build parl- + -iamo: parliamo. The stress moves onto the ending here (parlIAmo), unlike the singular forms.
The voi ending is the one place each class keeps its own vowel: -are gives -ate, -ere gives -ete, -ire gives -ite. So aspettare makes aspett- + -ate: aspettate.

prompt: Completa: 'Loro ____ in un ufficio in centro.' (lavorare)
explanation: For they, a regular -are verb takes -ano: lavor- + -ano gives lavorano. The stress stays back on the stem (laVOrano), which is why this antepenultimate-stress form is so often mis-said as lavoRAno.

prompt: Completa: 'Io ____ in Italia da molti anni.' (vivere)
explanation: The io ending -o is shared by every regular class, so an -ere verb behaves like an -are verb here: viv- + -o gives vivo. The classes only diverge in the other persons.
The tu ending -i is also shared across classes. From leggere you get legg- + -i: leggi. The double g keeps its soft sound before the -i.

translation.reveal: prendere - to take / to have (food, drink)

explanation: Here is where -ere parts company with -are: the second conjugation takes -e for he or she, not -a. So prend- + -e gives prende. Using prenda would borrow the -are ending by mistake.
The noi form is -iamo for every class, so scrivere matches parlare here: scriv- + -iamo gives scriviamo. The shared -iamo is one of the few endings you never have to choose between classes.
The voi ending carries the class vowel, and for -ere that vowel is -ete: cred- + -ete gives credete. Compare -are's -ate and -ire's -ite, which differ only in that vowel.

prompt: Completa: 'I turisti ____ il treno per Firenze.' (prendere)
explanation: The they form of -ere is -ono, not the -ano you would use for an -are verb: prend- + -ono gives prendono. Reaching for prendano is the classic cross-class slip, borrowing the first-conjugation ending.
Dormire is a third conjugation verb of the plain kind, so it takes ordinary endings with no insert: dorm- + -o gives dormo. Only the larger -isc- pattern adds -isc- here, and dormire is not in it.
A plain -ire verb takes -e for he or she, the same ending as -ere: sent- + -e gives sente. There is no -isc- here because sentire belongs to the plain third conjugation, not the -isc- pattern.
Plain -ire verbs share the -ono ending with -ere in the they form: part- + -ono gives partono. The -isc- pattern would give partiscono, but partire takes the plain stem.

prompt: Completa: 'A che ora ____ il negozio?' (aprire, voi)
explanation: The voi ending for the third conjugation is -ite, which sets -ire apart from -ere's -ete: apr- + -ite gives aprite. The 1pl and 2pl never carry -isc- in any -ire verb.

translation.reveal: seguire - to follow / to take (a course)

explanation: The noi ending -iamo is the same for every class. From seguire you keep the whole stem segu- and add -iamo: seguiamo. The u stays put because it is part of the stem, not a spelling adjustment.

When you say you do not understand something right now, you use the present indicative. Capire follows the -isc- pattern: the io / tu / lui / loro forms insert -isc- between stem and ending. So cap- + -isc- + -o gives capisco.
Pulire is an -isc- pattern verb, so the tu form carries the insert: pul- + -isc- + -i gives pulisci. The -sci is read soft, like the -sh- in English 'she'.
The lui / lei form of an -isc- verb adds the insert before the -e ending: prefer- + -isc- + -e gives preferisce. Without the insert you would get the non-existent prefere.
The they form of an -isc- verb is one of the four that take the insert: fin- + -isc- + -ono gives finiscono. The plain -ono without the insert (finono) is not a real form.
Spedire takes the -isc- insert in the io form: sped- + -isc- + -o gives spedisco. There is no rule that predicts which -ire verbs do this, so spedire is learned as an -isc- verb.

prompt: Completa: 'Loro ____ una nuova casa in campagna.' (costruire)
explanation: Costruire keeps its u in the stem and still takes the -isc- insert in the they form: costru- + -isc- + -ono gives costruiscono. The English progressive ('are building') maps onto the plain Italian present here.

Even though capire is an -isc- verb, the insert appears only in the four singular-and-they forms, never in noi or voi. So the noi form is just stem plus -iamo: cap- + -iamo gives capiamo. Carrying the insert in (capisciamo) is the classic over-extension.

prompt: Completa: 'Voi a che ora ____ di lavorare?' (finire)
explanation: The -isc- pattern is boot-shaped: it skips the noi and voi forms. So the voi of finire is the plain stem plus -ite: fin- + -ite gives finite. Inserting -isc- here (finiscite) wrongly extends the pattern.
The noi form drops out of the -isc- pattern, so preferire behaves like any plain verb here: prefer- + -iamo gives preferiamo. The insert belongs only to the io / tu / lui / loro corners.

translation.reveal: cercare - to look for / to search

explanation: Cercare has a hard c sound, and the tu ending -i would soften it in writing. To protect the hard sound, -care verbs add an h before the -i: cerc- + h + -i gives cerchi. Without the h, ceri would be read with a soft c.
Giocare keeps a hard c before the tu ending by inserting an h: gioc- + h + -i gives giochi. This h-insertion is purely a spelling device; it changes nothing in the other persons (gioco, gioca).
The noi ending -iamo also begins with a soft-making i, so -gare verbs add an h to keep the hard g: pag- + h + -iamo gives paghiamo. The h appears in exactly the two forms whose ending starts with -i.
Spiegare protects its hard g in the noi form with an h: spieg- + h + -iamo gives spieghiamo. Drop the h and spiegiamo would be read with a soft g, a different sound altogether.

The h is needed only when the ending begins with -i. The io ending is -o, which keeps the c hard on its own, so no h is added: cerc- + -o gives cerco. Adding the h everywhere is the over-application to avoid.

prompt: Completa: 'A che ora ____ a lavorare?' (cominciare, tu)
explanation: In cominciare the stem i only exists to soften the c. Once the tu ending supplies its own -i, the stem i is redundant and drops: cominc- + -i gives cominci, not the double-i comincii.

The g of mangiare is kept soft by the stem i, but the tu ending -i already does that job, so the stem i drops: mang- + -i gives mangi. Writing both i's (mangii) doubles a sound that is only pronounced once.
The noi ending -iamo already starts with i, so the stem i is again redundant and drops: mang- + -iamo gives mangiamo. The single i both softens the g and forms the ending.
Lasciare drops its stem i before the i-initial noi ending: lasc- + -iamo gives lasciamo. The sci keeps its soft sound with just one i doing the work.
The stem i only drops when the ending itself begins with i. The io ending is -o, so the stem i must stay to keep the g soft: mang- + i + -o gives mangio. Dropping it (mango) would harden the g.
In studiare the stem i is unstressed (you say STU-dio, not stu-DI-o). An unstressed stem i merges with the ending's i and only one is written: studi, not studii.
Inviare stresses its stem i (you say in-VI-o). A stressed stem i is kept, and the tu ending adds its own i, so both are written: invii. Reducing it to invi would lose the stressed vowel.
Sciare also stresses its stem i (sci-A-re, present SCI-o), so it is kept and the ending's i added: scii. The single-i form sci is the noun 'ski', not the tu verb form.

Essere is the most irregular verb in Italian, with forms that come from different roots. The io form is sono, learned whole rather than built from a stem. The same word sono also serves the loro form.
The tu form of essere is sei. There is no regular pattern to lean on here; essere's present is suppletive, so each person is memorised as its own word.
The lui / lei form of essere is è, written with a grave accent. The accent is not decorative: plain e is the word 'and', so 'è' (is) and 'e' (and) are different words that the accent keeps apart.
examiner_note: The accent is load-bearing: 'e' is the conjunction. The accent-input affordance is in the housing, so requiring è is fair.

explanation: The noi form of essere is siamo. Even the plural forms are suppletive: siamo shares nothing with the singular sono or sei, so it is held in memory as a separate shape.
The voi form of essere is siete. Watch the look-alikes: sete is the noun 'thirst', and siate is the subjunctive / imperative, not the plain present.
The loro form of essere is sono, the very same word as the io form. Context tells them apart: 'io sono' versus 'loro sono'. Note sonno (with double n) is the noun 'sleep', a different word.
Avere is the second auxiliary and is highly irregular. Its io form is ho, with a silent h that is never pronounced but is obligatory: it marks the verb off from o, the word 'or'.
examiner_note: The h is silent but obligatory; bare 'o' is the conjunction. No accent on ho.

translation.reveal: ragione - reason (avere ragione = to be right)

explanation: The tu form of avere is hai, again with the silent h. Drop it and ai is the articulated preposition 'to the', so the h is what keeps the verb distinct in writing.
The lui / lei form of avere is ha. The silent h separates it from a, the preposition 'to'. Italian uses avere, not essere, to give ages: 'ha vent'anni' is literally 'has twenty years'.

translation.reveal: fame - hunger (avere fame = to be hungry)

explanation: The noi and voi forms of avere have no h, because they look nothing like a shorter real word: noi abbiamo. The h appears only on the four forms (ho, hai, ha, hanno) that would otherwise collide with another word.
The voi form is avete, with no h. Adding an h here (havete) over-applies the rule: the silent h belongs only to the four forms that need disambiguating, not to the whole paradigm.
The loro form of avere is hanno, with the silent h and a double n. Without the h, anno is the noun 'year', so 'hanno' (they have) and 'anno' (year) are kept apart in writing by the h alone.

prompt: Completa: 'Ogni giorno ____ al lavoro in bici.' (andare, io)
explanation: Andare is one of a small group of high-frequency verbs with heavily contracted, irregular present forms. Its io form is vado, which abandons the stem and- entirely. These paradigms are learned whole, not derived.
The loro form of andare is vanno, built on the same irregular va- as the singular, not on the infinitive stem. The regular-looking andano does not exist.
Fare comes from Latin facere, and that older stem resurfaces in the io form faccio (with the double c). The contracted infinitive fare hides the fac- stem that the conjugation reveals.
The voi form of fare is the contracted fate, not the fuller facete you might expect from the fac- stem. Fare keeps a short 2pl, the same way dire keeps dite.

translation.reveal: mano - hand (dare una mano = to lend a hand)

explanation: The lui / lei form of dare is dà, written with a grave accent. The accent separates it from da, the preposition 'from': 'mi dà' (gives me) versus 'da Roma' (from Rome).
examiner_note: Accent is load-bearing: bare 'da' is the preposition.

translation.reveal: stare - to stay / to be (of health)

explanation: Stare patterns like dare and andare, with a contracted monosyllabic io form: sto. It needs no accent in the io form, and Italians use it for how someone is feeling: 'sto bene'.
The loro form of stare is stanno, with a double n, matching the shape of danno (dare) and vanno (andare). The single-n stano is not a form.

prompt: Completa: 'Non ____ dove sono le chiavi.' (sapere, io)
explanation: Sapere belongs to the same contracted group: its io form shrinks to a single syllable, so. It is written without an accent, and it means knowing a fact, as opposed to conoscere for knowing a person.
The loro form of sapere is sanno, with the same double-n ending as hanno, stanno and danno. The regular-looking sapono is never used.
Dire comes from Latin dicere, and the older stem dic- returns through most of its present. The io form is dico, built on dic- plus -o, not on the contracted infinitive.
Where the dic- stem would predict dicete, dire keeps a short, contracted voi form instead: dite. This is the one form that breaks the dic- pattern, the same way fare keeps fate.
The loro form returns to the dic- stem: dic- + -ono gives dicono. The look-alike dicano with an a is the subjunctive, not the plain present.
Bere is a contracted infinitive from Latin bevere, and the present opens it back out to the stem bev-. So the io form is bev- + -o: bevo, not the bero you would build from the short infinitive.
The expanded bev- stem runs through the whole present, so the tu form is bev- + -i: bevi. Once you know bere hides bev-, the endings themselves are the regular -ere endings.
The loro form keeps the bev- stem and takes the regular -ere ending -ono: bev- + -ono gives bevono. The same bev- drives the imperfect (bevevo) and the participle (bevuto).
A group of irregular verbs slips a -g- in before the ending in just two forms, the io and the loro. Venire is one: ven- + g + -o gives vengo. The -g- only appears where the ending starts with o.

prompt: Completa: 'I miei amici ____ alla festa stasera.' (venire)
explanation: The other -g- form is the loro: ven- + g + -ono gives vengono. Both the io and loro endings begin with o, which is the environment that triggers the inserted -g-.
The -g- shows up only in the io and loro forms, so the noi form has none: ven- + -iamo gives veniamo. Carrying the -g- in (vengiamo) over-extends it to a form that never takes it.
Tenere takes the same -g- insert as venire in the io form: ten- + g + -o gives tengo. The two verbs share the pattern, and the other persons (tieni, tiene) follow a diphthong shape.

translation.reveal: salire - to go up / to climb

explanation: Salire is a third-conjugation verb that still takes the -g- insert in the loro form: sal- + g + -ono gives salgono. Note it is not an -isc- verb, so saliscono is wrong on two counts.
Rimanere inserts the -g- in the io form like the rest of the group: riman- + g + -o gives rimango. The loro mirrors it as rimangono, while the other persons (rimani, rimane) drop the -g-.

prompt: Completa: 'La sera ____ con gli amici.' (uscire, io)

markpoints.label: uscire 1sg esco (stem vowel u to e)

translation.reveal: uscire - to go out / to exit

explanation: Some verbs change their stressed stem vowel in the forms where the stress falls on the stem. Uscire shifts u to e in the singular and loro: the io form is esco, not the regular-looking uscio.

markpoints.label: uscire 3pl escono (stem vowel u to e)

explanation: The loro form also carries the stem stress, so it shifts too: escono, not uscono. The e-stem appears in exactly the four forms (io, tu, lui, loro) where the stress sits on the stem.
In the noi form the stress moves onto the ending, so the stem reverts to its plain vowel: usc- + -iamo gives usciamo. Keeping the e-stem here (esciamo) is the high-frequency slip.
Sedere diphthongises its stressed stem vowel, turning e into ie: the io form is siedo. The diphthong appears under stress (siedo, siedi, siede, siedono) and disappears in sediamo.
With the stress on the ending, the diphthong collapses back to a plain e: sed- + -iamo gives sediamo. The ie only survives in the four stem-stressed forms, never in noi or voi.

prompt: Completa: 'Ho saltato il pranzo: ____ di fame!' (morire, io)

translation.reveal: fame - hunger (morire di fame = to be starving)

explanation: Morire diphthongises o into uo under stress, and the io form is muoio. Italians use it figuratively for being ravenous: 'muoio di fame'. The plain-stem moriamo loses the diphthong.

Dovere is a modal verb, and the three modals all have irregular present paradigms learned as wholes. Its io form is devo (the more formal debbo also exists). A modal is normally followed straight by an infinitive: devo studiare.
examiner_note: debbo is a fully standard, more formal variant of devo; accepted at full credit. Flagged in coverage as a register variant.

translation.reveal: potere - to be able to / can

explanation: Potere is the modal for being able to. Its io form is posso, with a double s and no trace of the infinitive's t. Like the other modals it leads into an infinitive: posso aiutare.

translation.reveal: potere - to be able to / can

explanation: The tu form of potere is puoi, with the diphthong uo. Note poi without the u is the adverb 'then / afterwards', a different word entirely.

translation.reveal: potere - to be able to / can

explanation: The lui / lei form of potere is può, written with an accent over the o. The accent marks the stressed final vowel of this monosyllable; the bare puo without it is incomplete.
examiner_note: Accent is load-bearing on può; bare 'puo' is wrong.

explanation: Volere is the modal for wanting. Its io form is voglio, with the gli sound. Unlike the other two modals, volere is often followed by a noun as well as an infinitive: voglio un caffè.
The loro form of volere is vogliono, keeping the gli sound of the singular voglio. The look-alike vogliano with an a is the subjunctive, not the plain present.
The noi form of dovere is dobbiamo, with a double b and an o where the infinitive has a u. The modals reshape their stems in the plural too, so dobbiamo is memorised rather than derived.

translation.reveal: chiamarsi - to be called / to call oneself

explanation: A reflexive verb pairs the verb with a reflexive pronoun that matches the subject. For io the pronoun is mi, placed before the conjugated verb: mi chiamo, literally 'I call myself'. The pronoun is what makes it 'my name is'.
examiner_note: One skill: the pronoun-plus-verb assembly. Single combined slot.

prompt: Completa: 'A che ora ____ la mattina?' (svegliarsi, tu)
explanation: For tu the reflexive pronoun is ti, and the verb takes its own tu ending: ti svegli. Both pieces have to agree with the subject, so a tu sentence needs ti and the -i verb form together.

prompt: Completa: 'Lei ____ le mani prima di mangiare.' (lavarsi)
explanation: For lui / lei the reflexive pronoun is si, before the verb: si lava. The pronoun marks the action as turning back on the subject, the difference between 'lava' (washes something) and 'si lava' (washes herself).

translation.reveal: alzarsi - to get up / to stand up

explanation: For noi the reflexive pronoun is ci, paired with the -iamo verb form: ci alziamo. The pronoun and the ending both carry the 'we' meaning, so they must match.
For loro the reflexive pronoun is si, the same word as the singular, paired with the -ano verb form: si lavano. Context and the verb ending tell the singular si lava from the plural si lavano.

prompt: Completa: 'Per la festa voi ____ sempre eleganti.' (vestirsi)

translation.reveal: vestirsi - to get dressed / to dress

explanation: For voi the reflexive pronoun is vi, with the third-conjugation -ite ending: vi vestite. As always the pronoun has to match the subject, so a voi sentence pairs vi with the voi verb form.

prompt: Complete: 'Ogni mattina ____ il giornale al bar.' (leggere, io)
explanation: 'Ogni mattina leggo il giornale al bar' means 'Every morning I read the newspaper at the café.' Something you do again and again as part of your routine is a habit, and Italian marks a habit with the plain present tense: leggo on its own covers English 'I read'. The habit marker ogni mattina tells you the sentence is about what regularly happens, so the present is the natural form; no past, future or stare + gerundio progressive is wanted.
examiner_note: Residual substring risk: 'leggo' sits inside 'leggono', so a wrong-person 'leggono' would false-credit. The (io) cue makes that attempt unlikely; accepted as residual.

prompt: Complete: 'In questa scuola le lezioni ____ ogni giorno alle otto.' (cominciare)
explanation: 'In questa scuola le lezioni cominciano ogni giorno alle otto' means 'At this school, lessons start every day at eight.' A statement about how things always are, a general truth, takes the plain present in Italian: cominciano states the standing fact. The markers in questa scuola and ogni giorno show nothing is happening at one particular moment, so the present is right and the progressive stanno cominciando would wrongly picture one specific eight o'clock unfolding now.
examiner_note: iniziano accepted as a full synonym; the usage skill is identical. Explicit plural subject (le lezioni), so no person cue per the cue-economy rule.

prompt: Complete: 'Di solito i negozi ____ alle nove e mezza.' (aprire)
explanation: 'Di solito i negozi aprono alle nove e mezza' means 'Shops usually open at half past nine.' What usually happens is a habit of the world around you, and habits sit in the plain present: aprono. Di solito ('usually') is the give-away that this is routine, not one morning in progress, so the present is wanted rather than the future apriranno or the progressive stanno aprendo.

prompt: Complete: 'Non posso uscire adesso: ____ la cena.' (preparare, io)

any_phrases.note: stare + gerundio is equally natural in this now-context; full credit per the context-dependent progressive policy

explanation: 'Non posso uscire adesso: preparo la cena' means 'I can't go out now: I'm making dinner.' Something going on at this very moment is a happening-now use, and Italian is happy to cover it with the plain present: preparo, where English must say 'I'm making'. Sto preparando (the stare + gerundio progressive) is equally correct here; the point to take away is that Italian never NEEDS the progressive the way English does, so the bare present is never too weak for a now-context.
examiner_note: Both preparo and sto preparando carry full credit by design: the context does not make the plain present clearly more natural, so per the ruling of 2026-07-14 no nudge-grading applies and the teaching lives in the explanation.

prompt: Complete: 'Dalla mia finestra ____ il mare.' (vedere, io)

markpoints.label: plain present with a perception verb

any_phrases.note: grammatical but unnatural: perception verbs resist the progressive; partial credit with the full explanation, per the context-dependent progressive policy

explanation: 'Dalla mia finestra vedo il mare' means 'From my window I can see the sea.' Seeing, hearing and the other senses describe a state of perceiving rather than an activity you perform, and verbs of this kind, stative verbs, sit in the plain present: vedo. Sto vedendo is not incorrect grammar, but Italians do not say it here, because perception is not an action in progress; if you wrote it, the answer is understandable but the plain vedo is what the language actually uses.

prompt: Complete: 'I nonni non possono rispondere adesso: ____ al telefono con la zia.' (parlare)
explanation: 'I nonni non possono rispondere adesso: parlano al telefono con la zia' means 'Grandma and grandpa can't answer right now: they're talking on the phone with our aunt.' This is a happening-now use of the present: the call is going on as we speak, and the plain parlano covers it fully, where English needs 'they're talking'. Stanno parlando is just as natural; Italian offers both, English only the progressive, and that asymmetry is the thing to learn.
examiner_note: 3pl chosen deliberately: the 3sg 'parla' is a substring of parlando/parlava/parlato/parlare, and positive any_phrases matches win over must_not_include, so a 3sg version of this item would false-credit wrong-tense attempts. 'parlano' has no such superstrings among plausible attempts.

explanation: 'Ho fame!' literally 'I have hunger', is how Italian says 'I'm hungry.' Physical states, hunger, thirst, heat, cold, sleepiness, fear, are felt in Italian as things you have, so the language reaches for avere in the present where English uses 'to be': avere fame, avere sete, avere freddo, avere sonno. The blank takes ho, the io form of avere; sono fame would be word-for-word English and is never said.
examiner_note: The verb choice is the test, so no lemma is cued. must_not 'sono' catches the essere calque; the deliberately absent lemma cue means a blank attempt is a genuine idiom gap, not a formation slip.

prompt: Complete: 'Mio fratello ____ dodici anni.' (He is twelve.)
explanation: 'Mio fratello ha dodici anni' means 'My brother is twelve years old.' Age in Italian is a state you have, not something you are: the pattern is avere + number + anni, literally 'to have twelve years'. So the blank takes ha, the 3sg present of avere; è dodici anni is an English calque and always wrong.
examiner_note: Residual substring risks: 'ha' sits inside 'hanno' (wrong-number attempt would false-credit; explicit singular subject makes it unlikely). Bare unaccented 'e' is NOT in must_not_include because a single letter would substring-match inside almost any answer; the accent guard is soft by house policy.

markpoints.label: plain present for a state of knowing

explanation: 'Non sappiamo ancora dove abita' means 'We don't know yet where he (or she) lives.' Knowing is not an activity you are in the middle of; it is a state you are in, and states sit in the plain present: sappiamo. Stative verbs like sapere, avere and volere resist the stare + gerundio progressive, so stiamo sapendo is not Italian at all. And before a question word like dove, knowing a fact is sapere, not conoscere.
examiner_note: must_not 'conosciamo' is safe here precisely because a clause follows: conoscere cannot take 'dove...', so the item pins sapere without testing vocabulary knowledge separately. 1pl chosen to avoid the 3sg-in-gerundio substring collision.

prompt: Complete the serving instruction: 'Questo vino si ____ freddo.' (servire)
explanation: 'Questo vino si serve freddo' means 'This wine is served cold' or 'You serve this wine cold.' Instructions about how things are done in general take the si-impersonal: si plus the verb in the third person of the plain present. With a singular subject (questo vino), the verb after si is 3sg: si serve. The 2sg servi is the informal you-voice of instructions and fine on its own, but after si it is wrong.
examiner_note: servire chosen deliberately over an -are/-ere verb: -ire 3sg forms ('serve') are not substrings of their own infinitive, future or 2pl, so the wrong-tense guards in must_not_include are all reachable. Only the gerundio ('servendo') contains 'serve', and 'si sta servendo' is not a plausible attempt here.

prompt: Complete the recipe step: 'Prima si ____ le patate a cubetti.' (tagliare)
explanation: 'Prima si tagliano le patate a cubetti' means 'First you cut the potatoes into cubes.' In the si-impersonal the verb agrees with the thing acted on: le patate is plural, so the verb goes plural too, si tagliano, as if the potatoes cut themselves. Keeping the singular (si taglia le patate) is the classic slip; when what follows si is plural, the plural noun drags the verb to -ano.
examiner_note: must_not entries 'taglia' and 'tagli' are substrings of the correct 'tagliano', which is safe: any_phrases are checked before must_not_include, so the correct answer credits first. A learner answering 'taglia' alone fails the any_phrases check and is then caught.

prompt: Complete the directions: 'Per il museo si ____ al secondo piano e poi a destra.' (salire)
explanation: 'Per il museo si sale al secondo piano e poi a destra' means 'For the museum you go up to the second floor and then turn right.' Directions in Italian often come as a statement of what one does, using the si-impersonal in the plain present: si sale, 'one goes up'. The verb after si sits in the 3sg: sale. The present does all the instructional work; no command form or future is needed.
examiner_note: salire chosen deliberately: its 3sg 'sale' is not a substring of salire/salirà/salite, so every wrong-form guard is reachable. 'salga' in must_not catches the Lei-imperative reach, a real temptation in a directions context. (Formation note: salire is a -go verb, but the 3sg 'sale' is regular, so no formation confound.)
